title: The Holographic Residual Stream
subtitle: Every Piece Contains the Whole — What If Transformers Work Like Holograms?

children.children: In a hologram, every fragment contains the entire image, just at lower resolution. What if the residual stream of a transformer works the same way? Every dimension carries information about every token and every relationship, encoded as interference patterns. This would explain superposition — why one vector can represent many concepts simultaneously — and why simple linear projections (attention queries and keys) can extract specific information from the same vector. This tutorial explores the analogy, its strengths, its limits, and what it might tell us about how meaning lives inside neural networks.

props.title: 1. How Holograms Actually Work

children.children: Before we can use holograms as a metaphor, we need to understand what they actually are — because the real physics is more interesting than the sci-fi version.

A photograph records intensity — how bright each point is. A hologram records interference patterns — the interaction between a reference beam and light reflected off an object. This interference pattern encodes both amplitude AND phase, which means it captures the full 3D structure of the wavefront.

props.title: The Key Trick: Interference

children.children: Imagine dropping two stones in a pond. The ripples overlap, creating a complex pattern of reinforcement (peaks meeting peaks) and cancellation (peaks meeting troughs). If you froze that pattern, you'd have a hologram of the two stone locations. From the frozen interference pattern alone, you could reconstruct where both stones were dropped — even though the pattern looks like random noise.

The critical property: the interference pattern is DISTRIBUTED. Information about stone A isn't located in one part of the pond and information about stone B in another. They're overlaid everywhere. Every region of the pond surface contains information about BOTH stones. If you cut the frozen pond in half, each half still contains both stone locations — just with more noise and less precision.

props.title: Break a Hologram in Half

children.children: This is the property that makes holograms magical: cut a holographic plate in half, and each half still shows the ENTIRE image, not half the image. Cut it into quarters — four complete images, each fuzzier than the original. Cut it into tiny pieces — each piece still shows the whole scene, just at lower resolution. The information isn't localized anywhere. It's everywhere at once.

This distributed encoding is made possible by the mathematical structure of interference: sine waves of different frequencies, superimposed. Sound familiar? It should — it's a Fourier transform. A hologram is literally the Fourier transform of the original image.

props.title: 2. The Residual Stream as Interference Pattern

children.children: Now let's look at a transformer's residual stream. At each token position, there's a vector — say, 1024 dimensions for GPT-2 Medium. This single vector must simultaneously encode:

• The token's own identity ('cat')
• Its part of speech (noun)
• Its syntactic role (subject)
• Its semantic features (animate, small, furry)
• Its relationship to other tokens in the sentence
• Discourse-level information (topic, register)
• Information needed to predict what comes next
• Probably dozens of other things we haven't identified

How does one vector carry all of this? The standard answer in mechanistic interpretability is 'superposition' — features are encoded in overlapping, partially-interfering directions in the vector space. But saying 'superposition' is basically saying 'hologram' with a different word.

props.analogy: Just as a holographic plate stores multiple 3D scenes as overlapping interference patterns in a 2D medium, the residual stream stores multiple semantic features as overlapping direction patterns in a fixed-width vector. Each 'feature direction' is like a reference beam — it interferes with the 'signal' to produce a pattern that's distributed across all dimensions.

props.title: Why This Isn't Just a Metaphor

children.children: The connection between holograms and distributed vector representations isn't new — it goes back to Karl Pribram's holonomic brain theory in the 1960s and Pentti Kanerva's hyperdimensional computing in the 1980s. The mathematical structure is identical: both use high-dimensional vectors where information is encoded in the relationships BETWEEN dimensions rather than in individual dimensions.

In a hologram, you recover a specific image by shining the right reference beam through the plate. In a transformer, you recover a specific feature by taking a dot product with the right projection vector (a query or key). The dot product IS the neural network equivalent of 'shining a reference beam' — it selectively amplifies the pattern that matches and averages out everything else.

props.title: 3. Attention as Holographic Readout

children.children: Here's where the analogy gets precise. In attention, a query vector Q asks: 'which other tokens have information relevant to me?' It does this by computing dot products with key vectors K at every position. The positions with high dot products get high attention weights, and their value vectors V get read out.

In holographic terms: Q is the reference beam. K is the interference pattern at each position. The dot product Q·K is the reconstruction process — it measures how much each position's holographic encoding matches the query's 'frequency.' High match = that position stored information at this frequency = read it out.

props.title: Multi-Head Attention = Multiple Reference Beams

children.children: A transformer doesn't use one attention pattern — it uses many heads (12-96 typically), each with its own Q, K, V projections. This is exactly like a multiplexed hologram — a single holographic plate that stores multiple images, each recoverable with a different reference beam angle. Head 1 might recover syntactic relationships (reference beam at angle θ₁), Head 2 might recover coreference chains (angle θ₂), Head 3 might recover semantic similarity (angle θ₃). The same residual stream vector, read with different projections, yields completely different information — just as the same holographic plate, illuminated from different angles, shows completely different images.

This explains something that seems almost magical about attention: how can the same vector simultaneously be a good key for syntactic queries AND semantic queries AND positional queries? Because the information is holographically encoded — distributed across all dimensions in overlapping patterns that are separable by the right projection.

props.title: 4. The MLP as Holographic Maintenance

children.children: In a real holographic system, interference patterns degrade. Noise accumulates. Patterns that should be separate start bleeding into each other. You need periodic 'cleanup' — reconstruct the signal, remove artifacts, re-encode.

This is exactly what the MLP might be doing. After attention reads from multiple positions and writes new information into the residual stream, the holographic encoding gets messy. Patterns that were cleanly separated start to interfere destructively. The MLP's job: clean up the encoding so downstream layers can still read it clearly.

Here's the connection to the 'Shooting the Swordsman' finding: if the holographic encoding is already clean — the interference patterns are well-separated and low-noise — then the MLP cleanup is unnecessary. You can replace it with a linear operation (or skip it entirely) and nothing breaks, because there were no artifacts to clean up. This is what we observe in ~88% of transformer layers.

props.title: When Cleanup IS Necessary

children.children: The ~12% of layers where linearization fails — Layer 3 of Pythia-1B, Layers 5-6 of Pythia-410M — might be layers where attention has created genuine holographic 'collisions.' Two features that previously occupied different regions of the interference pattern have been brought into the same region by the attention operation. Only a nonlinear operation can separate them again — you need the GELU to selectively suppress some components while keeping others, like a filter that passes certain frequencies and blocks others.

In holographic terms: these are the layers where two images stored in the plate have been illuminated simultaneously, and their reconstructions overlap. You need a nonlinear filter (GELU) to separate them. In most layers, the images don't overlap, so no filter is needed.

props.title: 5. The 'Break It In Half' Test

children.children: If the residual stream is truly holographic, it should pass the fundamental holographic test: every piece should contain the whole, at lower resolution.

Translated to neural networks: if you take a random subset of dimensions from the residual stream, you should still be able to recover ALL the encoded features — just with more noise. This is actually testable, and some existing evidence supports it:

props.title: Evidence: Linear Probes on Random Subspaces

children.children: Linear probing studies (Alain & Bengio 2017, and many since) consistently find that you can predict syntactic features, semantic features, and even factual knowledge from the residual stream using simple linear classifiers. Crucially, these probes work even on RANDOM projections of the residual stream to lower dimensions — you lose accuracy, but you don't lose categories of information. Part-of-speech, sentiment, topic — they're all still there in a random 100-dimensional slice of a 1024-dimensional stream. That's the holographic property: every piece contains the whole.

props.title: Evidence: Superposition in Sparse Autoencoders

children.children: Anthropic's work on sparse autoencoders (Bricken et al. 2023) found that a 512-dimensional residual stream can encode thousands of interpretable features in superposition. The SAE 'dictionary' that recovers these features is mathematically equivalent to a set of holographic reference beams — each one extracts a specific pattern from the interference. The fact that you need an overcomplete dictionary (many more features than dimensions) is exactly what holographic theory predicts: a holographic medium can store far more images than it has pixels, at the cost of some crosstalk between them.

props.title: 6. What the Holographic View Explains

children.children: If we take the holographic metaphor seriously, several puzzling properties of transformers become less mysterious:

props.title: Why Does Superposition Work At All?

children.children: The standard account of superposition says: there are more features than dimensions, so features must share dimensions. This is true but unsatisfying — WHY doesn't the sharing destroy information? The holographic answer: because the features are encoded as interference patterns at different 'frequencies' (different directions in high-dimensional space), and interference patterns can be superimposed without destroying each other, as long as the frequencies are sufficiently distinct. This is literally how multiplexed holograms work — hundreds of images on one plate, each at a slightly different reference angle.

props.title: Why Are Linear Probes So Effective?

children.children: It seems almost too good to be true that a simple linear classifier can extract complex semantic information from the residual stream. The holographic answer: a linear projection IS the readout mechanism for a hologram. It's not a coincidence that linear probes work — it's a consequence of the encoding scheme. The information was stored in a form that's specifically designed to be recoverable by linear operations. The transformer learned this encoding because attention (the primary computation) is itself a linear operation (weighted sum of values).

props.title: Why Does the Residual Stream Grow Across Layers?

children.children: The residual stream's norm typically grows as you go deeper in the network. In holographic terms: each layer's attention operation writes new interference patterns into the medium. The medium gets 'denser' — more information encoded, larger amplitude. This is exactly what happens when you expose a holographic plate to more images — the overall amplitude grows. The signal-to-noise ratio for any individual image decreases (each feature becomes harder to read out), but the total information content increases.

props.title: Why Can You Delete Entire Heads With Minimal Damage?

children.children: Ablation studies consistently show that you can delete individual attention heads — sometimes entire layers — with surprisingly little performance loss. The holographic explanation: the same information is encoded redundantly across multiple interference patterns. Delete one 'reference beam' (attention head) and others still carry the signal. This is the holographic robustness property — damage degrades gracefully rather than catastrophically, because nothing is stored in only one place.

props.title: 7. Where the Analogy Breaks

children.children: Every analogy has limits, and intellectual honesty demands we find them.

The holographic analogy is suggestive, not proven. The mathematical structure of interference patterns and high-dimensional superposition share deep similarities, but no one has demonstrated a rigorous isomorphism between holographic encoding and transformer representations. The following are genuine disanalogies, not quibbles.

props.title: Holograms Are Continuous; Transformers Are Discrete

children.children: A holographic plate records a continuous interference pattern. A residual stream vector has a fixed number of dimensions (1024, 2048). This limits the number of 'frequencies' available for encoding. In practice, this manifests as the superposition limit — at some point, adding more features causes destructive interference (crosstalk) that degrades all features. Real holograms have this limit too (it's set by the grain size of the film), but it's much less severe.

props.title: Holograms Are Static; the Residual Stream Evolves

children.children: A holographic plate is written once and read many times. The residual stream is written AND read at every layer — each layer modifies the encoding while simultaneously reading from it. This is more like a holographic medium that's being continuously re-exposed while you're trying to view it. The fact that this works at all is remarkable, and might be the hardest thing to explain under the holographic framework.

props.title: No Clear Equivalent of 'Reference Beam Angle'

children.children: In a real hologram, different images are stored at different reference beam angles, and the angular separation determines how much crosstalk there is. In the residual stream, the equivalent would be the angular separation between feature directions. Anthropic's SAE work suggests features DO have preferred directions, and 'almost-orthogonal' features can coexist with minimal interference. But the geometry is much messier than clean angular multiplexing — features aren't neatly arranged at regular angular intervals. The encoding scheme, if it IS holographic, is a very noisy, irregular hologram.

props.title: 8. From Metaphor to Mathematics

children.children: The gap between 'this is a nice analogy' and 'this is a theory' is bridged by testable predictions. Here are some the holographic view makes:

props.title: Prediction 1: Graceful Degradation Under Random Projection

children.children: Project the residual stream to k random dimensions (k < d). All features should remain recoverable, with accuracy scaling as √(k/d). This is the holographic signature: information loss is gradual and uniform, not catastrophic and feature-specific. If some features vanish entirely at k=d/2 while others are perfectly preserved, the encoding is NOT holographic — it's localized.

props.title: Prediction 2: Feature Crosstalk Scales with Feature Count

children.children: In a hologram, the noise floor rises as you store more images. The signal-to-noise ratio for each image goes as 1/√N where N is the number of stored images. If the residual stream is holographic, SAE reconstruction error should scale predictably with the number of active features at a given position. More active features = more crosstalk = more error. This is testable with existing SAE data.

props.title: Prediction 3: MLP Necessity Correlates with Encoding Density

children.children: If MLPs are doing holographic cleanup, they should be most necessary at layers where the encoding is densest (most active features, most interference). This predicts that the layers where linearization fails (Layer 3 of Pythia-1B, Layers 5-6 of Pythia-410M) should have higher SAE feature density than layers where linearization succeeds. This connects directly to the 'Shooting the Swordsman' paper — and it's testable.

The deepest version of this idea: the transformer didn't LEARN to use holographic encoding. Holographic encoding is what INEVITABLY EMERGES when you train a fixed-width residual stream to carry more information than it has dimensions, using linear readout operations (attention). The encoding scheme is forced by the architecture. The transformer had no choice but to become a hologram.

props.title: 9. The Pribram Connection

children.children: We'd be remiss not to mention Karl Pribram, the neuroscientist who proposed in the 1960s that the brain itself stores memories holographically. His 'holonomic brain theory' argued that neural firing patterns create interference-like patterns in dendritic networks, and that memory recall works by reconstructing these patterns — essentially 'shining a reference beam' through neural tissue.

Pribram's theory was controversial and never fully validated for biological brains. But it may have been accidentally right about artificial ones. The transformer architecture, with its fixed-width residual stream, linear attention mechanism, and superposition of features, implements something very close to what Pribram imagined — not in dendrites, but in vectors.

props.analogy: Pribram proposed: memories stored as interference patterns in dendritic fields, recalled by resonance with incoming stimuli. Transformers implement: features stored as superposed directions in the residual stream, recalled by dot-product attention with learned queries. The mathematical structure is nearly identical. The medium is different (neurons vs. floating-point vectors), but the encoding principle converges.

children.children: There's something poetic here: a theory about biological memory that couldn't quite be proven for brains may find its validation in artificial neural networks — not because we designed them to be holographic, but because holographic encoding is the optimal solution to the problem both systems face: store vast amounts of information in a fixed-capacity, distributed medium, and retrieve it with simple, local operations.

props.title: 10. So Is the Residual Stream a Hologram?

children.children: Probably not in a rigorous mathematical sense. The encoding is too messy, too irregular, too entangled with the specific learned weights. A real hologram has elegant Fourier structure; the residual stream has whatever structure gradient descent happened to find.

But as a framework for thinking about what's happening inside transformers, the holographic view is surprisingly powerful. It explains superposition without hand-waving. It explains why linear probes work. It explains why you can damage the network gracefully. It explains why MLP nonlinearity is mostly unnecessary. And it makes testable predictions.

Most importantly, it shifts our intuition in a productive direction. Instead of thinking of the residual stream as a 'container' that 'holds' features in specific locations, we think of it as a 'medium' where features exist as distributed, overlapping patterns. Nothing is anywhere in particular. Everything is everywhere at once. And the art of understanding transformers is learning to shine the right reference beam.
The next time someone asks 'where is the concept of CAT stored in this transformer?', try answering: 'Everywhere. It's an interference pattern. You just need the right beam to see it.'
